findings.title: Configured pseudo-slice names silently erase matched samples
findings.claim: Both implementations accept user slices named `(gc)` or `(uncollapsible)`, attribute ordinary samples to them, and then unconditionally remove those rows during rendering. The summary therefore reports matched time that has no corresponding slice.
findings.reproduction: Using one facts-v2 sample (`work`, `/app/x.rb`, value 7) and `slices: [{name: "(gc)", paths: ["/app"]}]`, I ran both `clankerprof slices` binaries. Both exited 0 with `{"slices":[],"summary":{"matching_pct":100.0,"matching_time_ns":7,"total_time_ns":7},"tool":"clankerprof_slices"}`. Repeating with `(uncollapsible)` produced the same wrong result. Validation checks duplicate/default names but never reserves the pseudo names.

findings.title: Zero-sum filtered samples use unrelated profile time as the percentage denominator
findings.claim: When filtered matching values cancel to zero, slice rendering substitutes the entire profile total as the denominator. This contradicts the documented matching-time denominator and produces arbitrary percentages for the filtered rows.
findings.reproduction: I used three samples: `/a` +10, `/b` -10, `/c` +5; slices A/B/default; and `--filter name:match`, selecting only the first two. Both CLIs exited 0 with `matching_time_ns: 0` and `total_time_ns: 5`, but reported A and its frame as `pct: 200.0` and B as `pct: -200.0`. The matching denominator is zero; the documented zero arm should not silently switch to the unrelated +5 sample.

findings.title: Zero-sum scope buckets discard their nonzero negative children
findings.claim: A rollup bucket whose positive and negative categories cancel is removed wholesale, including its nonzero negative category. This violates the normative rule that negative aggregate rows must render.
findings.reproduction: I ran `scopes` with one scope containing category Positive +10 and Negative -10, rolled up together as Work, plus an unrelated +5 profile sample. Both implementations exited 0 and emitted `{"boundaries":[{"buckets":[],"name":"S","samples":2,"total_time_ns":0,...}],"summary":{"total_time_ns":5,...}}`. Thus the +10 and -10 rows both disappear even though the specification explicitly requires negative rows to remain visible.

findings.title: A configured `Other` rollup makes projections emit reports rejected by compare
findings.claim: The scope loader permits a rollup named `Other`, while rendering independently appends an implicit `Other` bucket for leftover categories. This violates the stated invariant that projections never emit duplicate rows and makes a generated artifact invalid to the project's own compare command.
findings.reproduction: With category A=10, category B=5, and `rollup: {Other: [A]}`, both scope commands exited 0 and emitted two bucket objects named `Other` (times 10 and 5). Self-comparing either generated report then exited 2 with the exact envelope `{"error": "Duplicate Bucket row 'Other' in comparison input.", "ok": false}`.

findings.title: YAML merge keys silently change slice ownership between Python and Rust
findings.claim: Python expands YAML merge keys through PyYAML, while Rust leaves `<<` as an ordinary key. The same accepted slice file therefore performs different attribution.
findings.reproduction:
For a `/a/x` sample worth 7, I used `defaults: &defaults
  paths: ["/a"]
slices:
  - name: A
    <<: *defaults`. Python exited 0 with `"name":"A","is_default":false`; Rust exited 0 with the fallback `"name":"(all)","is_default":true`. Both claimed 100% matching, concealing the ownership change.

findings.title: Facts-v2 JSON acceptance diverges for lone UTF-16 surrogates
findings.claim: Python accepts a lone surrogate escape as a facts string and projects it, while Rust's strict JSON parser rejects the identical input. The shared JSON/facts input domain is therefore not actually shared.
findings.reproduction: I supplied facts-v2 whose strings array begins with the literal JSON escape `"\ud800"`. Python `slices` exited 0 and emitted a frame with `"function": "\ud800"`. Rust exited 2 with `{"error": "unexpected end of hex escape at line 1 column 145", "ok": false}`.

findings.title: An unknown focus name turns a real compare regression into success
findings.claim: The compare contract does not require any supplied focus name to exist. A typo or stale CI focus list therefore disables the gate instead of failing closed, while the report still shows the large delta as `stable`. This is a gamable requirements gap.
findings.reproduction: I compared slice `hot` changing from 10.0% to 50.0%. Without focus, both CLIs exited 2. With `--focus-slices typo`, both exited 0 and emitted `"has_regression": false` plus `{"name":"hot","delta_abs":40.0,"delta_rel":400.0,"status":"stable"}`. No warning or validation error identifies that the focus set matched zero rows.

findings.title: A valid large core-class CSV escapes Python's JSON error boundary
findings.claim: Python leaves `_csv.Error` outside its contracted exception boundary and imposes the csv module's default 131072-byte field limit, whereas Rust accepts the same CSV. This causes a traceback/exit 1 and parity failure on a valid input.
findings.reproduction: Using a one-sample facts profile and `targets --target parent --runtime ruby --core-classes` pointing to a CSV containing one 131073-character field, Python exited 1 with a traceback ending `_csv.Error: field larger than field limit (131072)`. Rust exited 0 and emitted a normal `parent`/`Other` target report with `time_ns: 1`. The specification requires contracted input failures to exit 2 with one JSON envelope, never a traceback.

findings.title: Valid large target totals break CSV and text byte parity
findings.claim: Python divides the exact integer before formatting elapsed time, while Rust first rounds the aggregate to f64. Valid u64-range totals can consequently round to different displayed values, contradicting the explicit CSV/text byte contract.
findings.reproduction: I projected two valid signed-i64 samples, 5000000000000000000 and 5717268054699998221, under target `parent` (valid aggregate 10717268054699998221). Both CSV commands exited 0, but Python emitted `10717268054699998221,178621134.24 min,100.00` while Rust emitted `10717268054699998221,178621134.25 min,100.00`. Text output likewise used `.24 min` versus `.25 min`.

findings.title: Rust's custom JSON float writer does not implement CPython repr
findings.claim: The Rust serializer assumes Rust scientific formatting supplies the same shortest digits as CPython, which is false. Valid projections therefore violate the normative byte-level JSON contract even when both implementations computed the same f64.
findings.reproduction: I used two valid samples, +5268900186379573 and -5268900186379173, producing a matching total of 400. Python `slices` emitted A/frame percentages `1317225046594893.2` and B `-1317225046594793.2`; Rust emitted `1317225046594893.3` and `-1317225046594793.3`. Both exited 0. This directly contradicts the required CPython `repr` spelling and byte parity.

findings.title: Repeated scalar CLI options have incompatible semantics
findings.claim: Python argparse keeps the last occurrence of ordinary scalar options, while Rust clap rejects duplicates except on specially annotated fields. The supposedly parallel command surfaces therefore accept different invocations.
findings.reproduction: `clankerprof facts --profile <empty> --profile <empty>` exited 0 in Python and emitted valid empty facts. Rust exited 2 with `the argument '--profile <PROFILE>' cannot be used multiple times`. Likewise, repeated `--threshold-abs 1 --threshold-abs 2` exited 0 in Python but 2 in Rust. Focus flags were explicitly standardized as last-wins, but the remaining scalar surface was not.

findings.title: Rust silently ignores a non-string scope `slices` path
findings.claim: The scope configuration's `slices` field is handled inconsistently: Python treats a present value as a path and fails, while Rust uses `as_str` and silently treats every non-string as absent. Invalid configuration can therefore produce an ordinary-looking Rust report.
findings.reproduction: With `slices: 123` and a scope named S over one sample worth 7, Python exited 2 (`{"error": "[Errno 9] Bad file descriptor: '/dev/fd/123'", "ok": false}` in the process-substitution reproduction). Rust exited 0 and emitted boundary S with `total_time_ns: 7`, having ignored the field entirely.

summary: 12 new issues were reproduced: 9 major and 3 minor. The mandated gates could not be executed in the read-only auditor sandbox: `./bin/dev check` failed when `mktemp` returned `Operation not permitted`, and `cargo test --workspace` could not open `target/debug/.cargo-build-lock` for the same reason. Reproductions used the existing Python and prebuilt Rust binaries without modifying the worktree.
